coredata.dc:title: Natural product-derived carbon dots: Applications as a fluorescent probe for detecting Ciprofloxacin and as an antimicrobial agent
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wanlin
Chen, Junlin
Yan, KangLi
Chi, Yuting
Cai, Shaoyu
Li, Xue
Pei, Shuchen
Chai, Shuiqin
Chen, Fang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a green and economical strategy to prepare carbon dots (E-CDs) based on Embryonic chrysanthemums was carried out. The prepared difunctional E-CDs showed high water-solubility and favorable photostability. The morphology and surface functional groups were analyzed by TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. Moreover, E-CDs possessed effective antibacterial activity. The minimum inhibitory concentrations of E-CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus are 0.93 mg/mL and 0.78 mg/mL, respectively. In addition, E-CDs are used as a fluorescent probe with a limit of detection of 0.101 μM (3σ/k) for ciprofloxacin and a linear range of 0–1.0 μM. Consequently, this work discovered a new, green, economical carbon dot with the potential application as a potent antibacterial agent and an effective fluorescent sensor of ciprofloxacin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Embryonic chrysanthemums
Carbon dots
Ciprofloxacin
Antibacterial
Fluorescent assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of E-CDs to CIP
Sensing mechanism of CIP detection using E-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Chongqing University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by Scientific Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (grant number KJQN201901501 ), Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Science and Technology (grant number ckrc2019031 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the E-CDs and the insets show the particle size distribution and the HRTEM images.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of E-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) O1s, (c) N1s.

(a) Excitation, emission spectra and UV–vis absorption of E-CDs. The insets show the photographs of the E-CDs under daylight lamps and under 365 nm UV radiation. (b) Emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths.
The generated ROS of E-CDs (a) Hydrogen peroxide and (b) Hydroxyl radicals. (c) Zeta potential distribution of E-CDs.
FL response to various (a) antibiotics, amino acids and (b) ions. (c) Effect of antibiotic mixtures on luminescence enhancement of E-CDs.

(a) The FL response of E-CDs to different ciprofloxacin concentrations, inset: FL intensity of E-CDs and E-CDs with addition of ciprofloxacin. (b) the relationship between ciprofloxacin concentration and (F – F0)/F0 , where F is the FL intensity in the presence of the target ciprofloxacin and F0 is the FL intensity in the blank group. Inset: linear relationship in the range of 0–1 μM.
(a) E-CDs Mechanisms for detecting ciprofloxacin. (b) UV -Vis absorption spectra of E-CDs, CIP and E-CDs/CIP.
Schematic illustration in this study. Synthesis and antibacterial properties of E-CDs from embryonic chrysanthemums and ciprofloxacin detection.

head.ce:title: Natural product-derived carbon dots: Applications as a fluorescent probe for detecting Ciprofloxacin and as an antimicrobial agent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing 401331, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing 401331, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a green and economical strategy to prepare carbon dots (E-CDs) based on Embryonic chrysanthemums was carried out. The prepared difunctional E-CDs showed high water-solubility and favorable photostability. The morphology and surface functional groups were analyzed by TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. Moreover, E-CDs possessed effective antibacterial activity. The minimum inhibitory concentrations of E-CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus are 0.93 mg/mL and 0.78 mg/mL, respectively. In addition, E-CDs are used as a fluorescent probe with a limit of detection of 0.101 μM (3σ/k) for ciprofloxacin and a linear range of 0–1.0 μM. Consequently, this work discovered a new, green, economical carbon dot with the potential application as a potent antibacterial agent and an effective fluorescent sensor of ciprofloxacin.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional spherical nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm [1]. The structure of CDs consists of a sp2/sp3 carbon core and a shell with some surface functional groups [2]. It was reported that nature carbon precursors [3–8] were prepared as versatile carbon dots through different methods. CDs have been emerging as one of nanomaterials for antibacterial agents, fluorescent sensing owing to the high water-solubility, stable fluorescence property, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility during past decades [9–13].

Antibiotics are drugs used to treat bacterial infections. The overuse and misuse of antibiotics in clinical, agricultural and livestock settings has led to the emergence of antibiotic resistance and drug-resistant bacteria [14]. It was reported that there were two antibacterial mechanisms of carbon dots, namely, cationic properties and reactive oxygen species (ROS) [15]. A good deal of positively charged carbon dots can be synthesized via cationic compounds rich in nitrogen-containing groups [16]. It works in the way of combination between positively charged carbon dots and negatively charged bacteria, which can break down the membrane integrity of bacteria and disintegrate the cytomembrane [17]. The ROS include superoxide (•O2−), hydroxyl radical (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and singlet oxygen (1O2) and excessive ROS cause oxidative damage to significant biological macromolecules such as lipids, proteins and deoxyribonucleic acid [18].

Furthermore, CDs have been successfully applied to fluorescent probes due to high quantum yield, favorable photostability, and robust chemical inertness [19]. Ciprofloxacin, a quinolone antimicrobial agent, is mainly used for genitourinary tract infections caused by sensitive bacteria. Excessive use can cause skin, gastrointestinal tract, liver, nervous system and other adverse reactions. Therefore, it is of great practical significance to prepare a selective and sensitive probe for the quantitative determination of ciprofloxacin. The fluorescent probes, used for the detection of ciprofloxacin, have the advantages of simple operation without sophisticated instruments. Carbon dots have been applied to fluorescent probes because of their low toxicity, environment-friendly and light-resistant bleaching compared with the traditional materials.

Embryonic chrysanthemums as a precursor are cheaper, safer and easier to obtain compared with toxic organic molecules. Herein, we converted Embryonic chrysanthemums into fluorescent carbon dots (E-CDs). in an economical and green method-hydrothermal method for the first time (Scheme 1 ). The morphology and surface functional groups of the E-CDs were observed by TEM, FTIR and XPS. Moreover, not only the E-CDs could be used to inhibit the growth of bacteria, but also could it be detection of by Ciprofloxacin. This research provided a new way for multifunctional fluorescence carbon dots and a prospective application in clinic and environmental analysis.

Embryonic chrysanthemums were purchased from supermarket (Chongqing, China). Tri(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) and 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (HPA) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS), Luria-Bertani (LB) medium, coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (CCA), 3-(4,5- Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenylte-trazolium bromide (MTT) and CIP were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment. E. coli JM109 and S. aureus ATCC 6538 were from our laboratory. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used in accordance with the standards.

The morphology of the E-CDs was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) using a JEOL 2100F (Tokyo, Japan). Elemental analysis of the E-CDs were performed using an ESCALAB Xi+ X-ray photoelectron n spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) image was recorded on a Nicolet iS5 FT-IR (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc., Waltham, MA, USA) with 32 scans from 610 to 4000 cm−1 using the KBr method with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The fluorescence (FL) spectra were measured on a Shimatzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrum was monitored with an Evolution 220 (Thermo Electron, USA) UV-visible spectrometer using ultrapure water as solvent. MIC assay was carried out by using a microplate reader from Awareness Technology (Florida, USA) at 600 nm. The Zeta potentials of E-CDs and bacteria were recorded with by Nano Brook 90Plus Malvern laser particles size analyzer.

The E-CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal synthesis method. Firstly, the embryonic chrysanthemums were dried at 100 °C for 5 h, removed and then ground to powder. Subsequently, 0.30 g of embryonic chrysanthemums powder and 20 mL of ultrapure water were transferred into a 50 mL autoclave lined with Teflon and heated at 180 °C for a period of 6 h. The obtained suspension of E-CDs was naturally cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, then filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 μm). The product obtained by centrifugation and filtration was dialysed in a 500 Da dialysis bag having ultrapure water for 12 h and the dialysate was changed hourly. Finally, the purified E-CDs were freeze-dried to obtain solid powder and stored refrigerated at 4 °C prior to use.

E. coli and S. aureus were used as representatives of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria to assess the antimicrobial activity of the E-CDs. Two bacterial strains were cultured in LB medium at 37 °C overnight under shaking at 180 rpm. The OD600 value of the bacterial suspension was measured with an enzyme marker, and the incubation was stopped when the OD600 was 0.6–0.8, and the final bacterial concentration was adjusted to 1.5 × 107 CFU/mL by LB dilution.The diluted bacterial suspension was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

Dilution of carbon points to different multiples by LB. The diluted bacterial suspension and different concentrations of carbon points were added to 96-well plates in a 1:1 ratio, with a total of 200 µL suspension in a well. The 96-well plate was incubated at 37 °C for 12 h and the opacity of each well was measured using a microplate reader. MIC was the lowest concentration where no bacterial growth was observed. Then 10 µL of MTT was added to each well and incubated for another 20 min.

The growth trend of bacteria under different concentrations of E-CDs solution was named as bacteriostatic curve. Each strain (1 × 106 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL) was inoculated with different concentrations of carbon dots in 10 mL centrifuge tubes, and incubated with shaking at 37 °C for 24 h. The OD600 values of the treated bacterial samples were measured by a microplate reader at the indicated times.

1 mL of activated bacterial suspension were mixed well with 1 mL of diluted E-CDs solution and incubated in an incubation shaker at 180 rpm at 37 °C for 12 h. The zeta potential of the incubated suspension was then measured. The ability of E-CDs to produce hydrogen peroxide and hydroxyl radicals was evaluated by fluorescence spectrophotometry.

The E-CDs were excited by 330 nm continuous UV lamp for 60 min to access its photobleaching performance. FL intensity changes were obtained through the pH range of 1.89 to 11.98 and high concentrations of H2O2 (0.6 mM) and NaCl (4 mM)solutions, which were used to evaluate the optical stability of E-CDs in complex environments.

To investigate the selectivity of E-CDs for various antibiotics, amino acids and ions. In a standard operations, various antibiotics, amino acids and ions stock solutions were prepared for concentrations of 500 µM using deionized water. The maximum FL intensity was recorded as F0 by taking 700 μL of 8-fold diluted E-CDs in a quartz cuvette (Blank). Following the addition of each antibiotic, amino acids and ions, the E-CDs solution was stirred as normally. After incubating it for 1 h, FL spectra were recorded under excitation at 330 nm. In addition, sensitivity experiments were carried out at lower concentrations (0–80 µM) for highly sensitive antibiotic (CIP). The experiments were repeated 3 times to ensure the accuracy of the data. All the above experiments were carried out at room temperature.

TEM and XPS spectroscopy were used to characterize the as-prepared E-CDs. The particle size and morphology of E-CDs was shown in Fig. 1 , and the TEM image showed that E-CDs were spherical shape with good dispersion and size uniformity. The particle size ranges from 3.15 to 7.83 nm, and the average diameter was 5.22 nm. In addition, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1, it also had a clear graphite lattice structure, with a lattice stripe spacing of 0.20 nm, which was the same as the (102) diffraction surface of graphene [20].

The elemental composition and atomic percentage of E-CDs were analysed by XPS to further determine the functional groups on the E-CDs surface. As depicted in Fig. 2 (a), three peaks appeared in the XPS spectra of E-CDs for carbon, nitrogen and oxygen with elemental content of 55.38 %, 18.42 % and 26.20 % respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the O1s spectrum presented three single peaks, corresponding to 530.9 eV (CO), 531.6 eV (OCN) and 532.7 eV (COH) respectively. N1s showed three single peaks of 399.4 eV ((R-NH2), 400.3 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.4 eV (quaternary N) in Fig. 2(c). As shown in Fig. S1, The high-resolution C1s XPS had absorption peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.1 eV, corresponding to CC, CO/CN and CO/OCO, respectively [21,22]. The above analysis results indicate that indicating the possible presence of OH, quaternary amine, amide or -NH2 and -COOH on the surface of E-CDs.

FTIR was used to explore the surface groups of synthesized E-CDs as shown in Fig. 3 . The absorption peaks at 2930 and 671 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration and the bending vibration of CH, respectively. The absorption peak at 1390 cm−1 can be attributed to the CN stretching vibration or the bending vibration of OH. The peaks at 3390 and 1610 cm−1 are caused by NH stretching vibrations and NH bending vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 3180, 1670, and 1070 cm−1 are caused by OH, CO, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [23–25]. The FTIR and XPS results were in agreement, confirming that the surface of E-CDs was enriched with a variety of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups.

As demonstrated in Fig. 4 (a), the E-CDs solution shows a colorless under daylight lamps (insets, left) and pale blue color fluorescence under 365 nm UV radiation (insets, right). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum shows a typical absorption peak at 300 nm for E-CDs, which can be attributed to the n-π* jump at the carbon lattice edge [26].

As shown in Fig. 4(a), the optimal excitation and emission wavelength for E-CDs were 330 nm and 442 nm, respectively. The E-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence in the range of 250 ∼ 410 nm in Fig. 4(b). Moreover, the prepared E-CDs showed excellent stability. As shown in Fig. S2–S3, the FL intensity of the E-CDs did not change much. The results showed that the E-CDs had good resistance to photobleaching, acid-base, salt and oxidation.

The microdilution method was used to assess the bacteriostatic activity of E-CDs against Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus. MTT staining could detect live cells to indirectly reflect the value-added activity of the cells. The bacterial suspensions of both bacteria were treated with different concentrations of E-CDs and embryonic chrysanthemums solution, respectively, and incubated for 12 h before MTT staining in Figs. S4–S5. The MICs of E-CDs for E. coli and S. aureus were 0.93 mg/mL and 0.78 mg/mL, respectively.The MICs of embryonic chrysanthemums solution for E. coli and S. aureus were 1.55 mg/mL and 1.16 mg/mL, respectively. The results showed that E-CDs exhibited better inhibitory activity against both bacteria compared to embryonic chrysanthemums solution, with stronger inhibitory activity against S. aureus in Fig. S4(b) than E. coli in Fig. S4(a).

Based on the MIC results, the bactericidal activity of E-CDs against both bacteria was evaluated in one step using the plate dilution method and inhibition curves. As shown in Fig. 5 , When the bacteria were treated with E-CDs at a concentration of 0.5 MIC, the growth of both bacteria was inhibited to a certain extent; when the concentration of E-CDs reached at 1 MIC, the growth of both bacteria were significantly inhibited; and when the concentration of E-CDs reached at 2 MIC, the growth of both bacteria were almost completely inhibited. As depicted in Fig. S6, when E-CDs concentrations reached 1 MIC and 2 MIC, the growth of both bacteria completely stopped within 24 h. The E-CDs inhibited S. aureus in Fig. S6(b) better than E. coli in Fig. S6(a), in agreement with the MTT staining results.

To further investigate the antibacterial mechanism of E-CDs, we explored whether the ROS produced by E-CDs and the charge distribution on the surfaces of E-CDs and bacteria. The oxidation of 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (HPA) by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase yields fluorescent dimers (λem = 450 nm) that can be used as a reporter assay for hydrogen peroxide production in solution [27]. It was added to the E-CDs solution, and under light as shown in Fig. 6 (a), the FL intensity at 450 nm weakened, and ten minutes into a downward trend, proving that the E-CDs produced H2O2. Hydroxyl radicals oxidize coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (CCA) to fluorescent 7‑hydroxy-coumarin-3-carboxylic acid [27]. In the presence of E-CDs in Fig. 6(b), the FL intensity at 450 nm was weakened, and the tendency of weakening of FL intensity increased with the increase of treatment time. It suggested the production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) by E-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the zeta-potential of E-CDs was + 6.07 eV, and the zeta-potentials of E. coli and S. aureus were – 14.13 eV and – 12.47 eV, respectively. In addition, E-CDs treated the zeta-potentials of E. coli and S. aureus were – 12.07 eV and – 9.46 eV, respectively. It verified that the existence of electrostatic interactions between E-CDs and bacterial cells. The positive charge of E-CDs were mainly derived from surface nitrogen-rich nitrogen-containing groups, as shown in Fig. 2.

The above results demonstrated that the antimicrobial mechanism of E-CDs against bacteria was through electrostatic interactions, which promotes the penetration of small-sized E-CDs into bacteria and the generation of ROS to induce bacterial oxidative stress, thus destroying the bacterial cellular structure and leading to cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of E-CDs to CIP
ce:section.ce:para: Testing the selectivity of E-CDs for various antibiotics, amino acids, and ions, we occasionally found potential applications for fluorescence sensing of E-CDs: specific fluorescence enhancement for CIP. As shown in Fig. 7 (a,b), only E-CDs treated with CIP showed a significant fluorescence enhancement response, and the FL intensity of E-CDs treated with other antibiotics (namely Metronidazole, Doxycycline, Glucose, Amoxicillin, Furazolidone, Chloramphenicol, Streptomycin, Roxithromycin, Terramycin, and Tetracycline), amino acids (namely Lysine, Arginine, Proline, Alanine, Valine, Glycine, Tryptophan, Leucine, and Phenylalanine) and ions (namely Cu2+, Al3+, K+, Sn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Na+, NH4 +, Co2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, C2H2O2 −, PO4 3−, F−, NO3−, IO3 −, Cl−, S2−, Br−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, and CN−) did not change significantly. This suggested that E-CDs have good selectivity for CIP. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 7(c), the effect of the mixture of antibiotics on CIP detection was negligible, indicating that E-CDs were highly selective for CIP.

In order to examine the sensitivity of CIP, we chose to treat E-CD with CIP at concentrations ranging from 0–80 μM, respectively, and measured its FL intensity after sufficient reaction. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the FL intensity of the present target CIP (insets, left) was stronger than that of the blank group (insets, right) and the FL intensity was gradually enhanced with the increase of CIP concentration. As shown in Fig. 8(b), the enhancement efficiency showed an excellent linear relationship with CIP concentration with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9992 in the range of 0 - 1.0 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.101 μM based on the formula 3σ/ k (σ is the standard error of the intercept, and k is the slope of the linear plot).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of CIP detection using E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The enhancement in FL intensity of E-CDs were ascribed to the hydrogen bonding and electronic transfer between E-CDs and CIP. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), the surface of E-CDs were rich in OH, NH2 and COOH, which had good water solubility and can bind to CIP through hydrogen bonding. Meanwhile, E-CDs and CIP could be connected by a conjugated system, which was attributed to the electron-accepting nature of quaternary amines on E-CDs and the electron-releasing effect of CIP. In addition, in Fig. 9(b), the shoulder peaks of E-CDs/CIP produce a red shift compared to E-CDs and CIP, verifying the existence of a conjugate effect between E-CDs and CIP. Thus, the synergistic effect of hydrogen bonding and electron transfer resulted in the enhancement of FL intensity of E-CDs with CIP concentration [28].

In this work, a green and economical strategy to prepare carbon dots (E-CDs) based on Embryonic chrysanthemums was carried out. The prepared difunctional E-CDs showed high water-solubility and favorable photostability. The morphology and surface functional groups were analyzed by TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. E-CDs had amorphous nature with no crystal lattice and the average diameter of 5.22 nm. Some functional groups like nitrogen groups, oxygen groups, and silicon groups were discovered. Moreover, E-CDs possessed effective antibacterial activity. The minimum inhibitory concentrations of E-CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus are 0.93 mg/mL and 0.78 mg/mL, respectively. In addition, E-CDs are used as a fluorescent probe with a limit of detection of 0.101 μM (3σ/k) for ciprofloxacin and a linear range of 0–1.0 μM. Consequently, this work discovered a new, green, economical carbon dot with the potential application as a potent antibacterial agent and an effective fluorescent sensor of ciprofloxacin.
Data will be made available on request.

Wanlin Sun: Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Junlin Chen: Formal analysis, Software. KangLi Yan: Data curation. Yuting Chi: Methodology. Shaoyu Cai: Formal analysis, Investigation. Xue Li: Project administration. Shuchen Pei: Supervision. Shuiqin Chai: Formal analysis, Investigation. Fang Chen: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Scientific Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (grant number KJQN201901501), Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Science and Technology (grant number ckrc2019031).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.137349.
